body_txt: The Hellevator is an elevator that serves as a bridge between the world of the living and that of the dead. It provides a clear cue, particularly visual fiction, that the riders are going deep beneath the surface of the Earth to the underworld. When an elevator goes down for several hundred floors, it's pretty difficult to come to any other conclusion as to where it's going. The concept of a mystical hell-travelling elevator was introduced in the early days of film, when automatic elevators were a new invention. Previously, elevators were controlled by trained employees who rode with passengers in the cab, and many people weren't comfortable riding in an elevator that could move of its own accord. Additionally, an enclosed elevator provides a sense of being trapped as it descends — you can always stop going down a tunnel or staircase, or even climb back up an escalator, but if the elevator starts plunging down you can't do anything but wait until it opens... and pray. Animated works will sometimes depict the Hellevator as an escalator or a roller coaster. Expect to see floor numbers passing by at an increasingly rapid rate, only pausing to blink on the six a few times . Not to be confused with the Hellevator, the lunar-based Space Elevator in Schlock Mercenary or the TV series. See Stairway to Heaven for its sister trope (usually, there are stairs or an escalator leading to Heaven, but an elevator to Hell for some reason — perhaps suggesting it's much easier to end up in the latter ); Evil Elevator for when the elevator itself tries to kill you; and Hellmouth , for a more... organic means of travel to the big fire below. May head to wherever somebody Dug Too Deep .
